item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The "magic conch" of The Foundation. A naturally humorous anomaly that isn't a joke SCP is a rarity.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting log of failed neutralizations, that make some relatively bad older SCPs more interesting with said log. +1(though maybe it makes more sense in tales than a mainline SCP, but whatever it is what it is.

item.title: Important One Day Edit to Documentation
item.content:encoded: There's a reason I never tried stand-up

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this could work as a joke, if you add a real punchline to it.

To be in the mainlist, it needs something more to it. It doesn't need to be more serious, just have more meat.

Since the Foundation doesn't neutralize things, this line of thought isn't interesting by itself. The very idea that this think was tested falls bad, it would be more believable that the Foundation simply don't know what the results would truly be because they won't try those things anyway.

What it could bring as a mainlister, or even a -J, would be an exploration of some character. Hints about the author's nature and/or intent, effects on some personnel that comes to know of it, it being targeted by some GOI for a reason or another, even by mistake.

You can do something with it, instead of relying solely on the skip. Or the collab, that was based on a faulty premise, anyway, as interesting as it seems to make space for people to put neutralizations and funny backfires.

item.title: Wikiwalk
item.content:encoded: I've decided to link all the task forces I use in SCPs to MaliceAforethought's excellent list, starting with this one. I think having a linked-to reference page for MTFs will make them more consistent and give new authors a valuable resource.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a good answer too. There are (presumably) safe ways to neutralize SCPs, but 2305 won't provide any.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It is also pretty hard for me to believe that all the skips involved will all backfire or cause some scene, feels to contrivied, especially with all the logs.

As an alternitive to the Author's Official Response above, you're assuming that the SCP isn't lying it's arse off, or that it's not specifically describing scenarios that WILL backfire. SCPs are like hazardous materials; there's a right way to dispose of them, and there's the "the town's cancer rates go skyhigh and the federal government throws the book at you while you become a mass media pariah" way of disposing them. If you don't know how a SCP works, are you sure you want to dispose of it in a seemingly logical manner?

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded: SCP Foundation is now just "Foundation", since that seems to be the more common in-universe term.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, it's rare for someone to submit a detailed critique of one of my SCPs (frankly, they're not that well-known or important; djkaktus or Kalinin I am not), so I should do you the courtesy of responding.

Full disclosure: this article is, at its core, not to be taken too seriously. It's a silly idea I had that was positively received because of the creative potential in the collab log. Really, the collab log was the main reason I made it.

Rebuttal
- Hopefully a satisfactory one.

I personally feel like this skip is very fundementally flawed in that, the one leaving comments, or whoever made the skip, is making fun of the Foundation for neutralizing skips, which the Foundation doesn't really do…? (They do in extreme situations, but judging by the article, I can assume the skip is set in where the Foundation still almost always contains things, not destroys things.)

It's not really taunting them for neutralizing SCPs. It's taunting them by showing them methods to neutralize SCPs that don't work. Basically going "hey, you know how you think neutralizing SCPs could work sometimes? Well ya can't, buddy. This is what happens when you do. LOL"

Essentially, it's someone fucking with the Foundation for the lulz. I can understand that not working for ya.

The collab logs on the other hand, derails significantly, with increasingly contrived way of anomly backfiring. The author's encourgement to create catostrophic backfire results didn't help either. The comments also become more and more hostile towards the Foundation, or straight up bashing for the non-existent neutralization effort, and are not the snarky comments the main skip has.

I admit I haven't looked at the collab log for awhile, and have mostly relied on the mods to keep it pruned with occasional veto power. In retrospect a collab log should probably have been more regulated by me to prevent newbies from spamming it with dumb shit.

It is also pretty hard for me to believe that all the skips involved will all backfire or cause some scene, feels to contrivied, especially with all the logs.

Not sure how to address this. At the time I wrote this (still today?), the standard line was "the Foundation contains, the GOC destroys, and the latter is bad because anomalies are unpredictable." When you try to destroy an SCP, it usually backfires, so this was just a logical extension of that. Basically, an entity that knows destroying SCPs doesn't work, has knowledge of what will happen if it is tried, and shares that knowledge for trolling purposes.

Thanks for the critique. Honestly, this premise is really stupid, so you're either going to love it or hate it. I thought I got what I could out of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I personally feel like this skip is very fundementally flawed in that, the one leaving comments, or whoever made the skip, is making fun of the Foundation for neutralizing skips, which the Foundation doesn't really do…? (They do in extreme situations, but judging by the article, I can assume the skip is set in where the Foundation still almost always contains things, not destroys things.)

And because that bit doesn't make sense, the entrys doesn't really work for me, even though they are actually pretty well written. The collab logs on the other hand, derails significantly, with increasingly contrived way of anomly backfiring. The author's encourgement to create catostrophic backfire results didn't help either. The comments also become more and more hostile towards the Foundation, or straight up bashing for the non-existent neutralization effort, and are not the snarky comments the main skip has.

It is also pretty hard for me to believe that all the skips involved will all backfire or cause some scene, feels to contrivied, especially with all the logs. And if one were to argue that the creator of the anomaly is a flawed character ans don't realize the lapse in their own logic… I think that only works maybe if the neutralization attempts doesn't fail as predicted, or something like that, right now they don't seem to be potrayed as a flawed.

Downvote for both the skip and the collab log. Feel the need to leave this comment because I don't see anyone in discussion touching on this. Apologies if I had over looked such comments.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
According to the document, similarly.

Otherwise, write it up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know. But, what does it mean by an identical fashion? Like, for instance, how would 139's termination attempt fail if really attempted?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2305 has no effect on reality; all documented neutralization attempts appear to be completely fictional. However, if an attempt is made at neutralizing the SCP object subject to 2305-A documentation using the method described, said attempt will fail in a near-identical fashion as described in the 2305-A documentation.Minor variations on the neutralization method have yet to yield successful results, despite numerous attempts…

Also, there are several non-K scenario-failures in the extended log, like the ones for SCP-2282 and SCP-3098.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is an interesting one. But, how does the neutralization attempt backfire? They have all depicted the end of the world. So, how do we know that attempting these neutralization attempts backfires in the same way if just one attempt ends the world?

item.title: Re: Hmm...
item.content:encoded:
pastarasta1, not only is that comment pretty immature, it actually doesn't work as a punchline because someone needs to have said "under where" and it's more of an out-loud pronunciation gag than a readable joke.

this has been your daily dose of #nofunzyn

item.title: Re: Hmm...
item.content:encoded:
it actually doesn't work as a punchline because someone needs to have said "under where" and it's more of an out-loud pronunciation gag than a readable joke.

i thought i was being clever as fuck damn, im a idiot.

in ur underwear XDDDD

item.title: Re: Hmm...
item.content:encoded: Where?

item.title: Hmm...
item.content:encoded: This has "Gamers Against Weed" written all over it.
Congrats, TOTALY USELESS ideas paper! You deserved it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was too unprofessional for me to like. It should be moved to a joke site.
-1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Horrible, horrible necroposting, I know. I won't do it again.

But the idea of shouting "Bad reality! Stop that right now!" as if the Universe was a dog is absolutely hilarious, and now I want to write a tale about a frustrated O5 writing one of those letters you never intend to send addressed to reality, politely asking it to quit the bullshit.

( ͡° ͜ʖ ͡°)

Collab log is still open, BTW.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Hobbes is real"

Hobbes… THE Hobbes?
Calvin was right all along

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: goddammit that last one. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this is the longest I've ever spent reading a single article, and I was pretty fascinated the whole way through. I guess that's partly because of the nature of linking to so many other articles, but you add enough that it's justified.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the idea, but it wasn't presented in an interesting way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was born to be a collab, that's for sure.
I think this is a fun way to let people play with crosstesting, SCP destruction and the like, without cluttering the main list with junk articles.
+1 for sure, and I'll be checking back periodically for quite some time.

item.title: Clarification
item.content:encoded:
The definition of "failure" here means that the SCP was not neutralized without any negative consequences. So even if the SCP was neutralized, if it creates new anomalous phenomenea that make the situation worse, that counts as a failure.

Realized the wording was somewhat ambiguous so I wanted to clear that up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Once I thought of 2740, I had to use it for the last log.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am really easy to please.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this for two reasons:

It's predictive, but not useful, other than to say "don't do this".

It's aware of the Foundation and messing with them, but otherwise doesn't seem to have a purpose. If this had been linked to anartists, AWCY or otherwise, I don't think I'd have liked it at all.

As it stands, I think I might want to give the collab document a whirl. :3

item.title: Collab Log Added!
item.content:encoded:
http://www.scp-wiki.net/scp-2305-collab

Go forth and add lulzy failed neutralizations.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Will add it as soon as I can think of a few more. If you have any ideas, PM them to me and I will add them to the new page.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I found the commentary on the logs intensely annoying, but ….I think that might have been the point?
No vote???

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted because for me the crosslinks to lesser-known articles help to tie the Foundation universe as a whole together and make the article itself surprisingly sinister. I like the descriptions of the failed neutralizations, and while on reflection those could have just been spun off into a tale I think having them be part of an actual SCP makes everything fit together better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Plus, I appreciate how the crosstests are mostly from series 2 and 3, rather than the usual 173, 682, 076, etc.

That was a deliberate choice. If we're going to make crosslinking non-taboo, we should do it in a modern way and bring attention to more obscure skips in the process.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it. It's weird, a little random and more than a little meta, but in a good way. There's some 4th wall leaning with the implication that maybe there's something outside looking in on the Foundation and it wants to mess with them. Plus, I appreciate how the crosstests are mostly from series 2 and 3, rather than the usual 173, 682, 076, etc.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Was a little back and forth on whether I liked this or not, but ultimately upvoted because it fits with my headcanon of facepalm-inducing "Bad reality. Stop that." stuff occurring in and around foundation sites all the time. (IE No one made this thing. It just showed up in that locker at some point, possibly with its own registration paperwork filled out.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Doesn't grab me. There's no logic or narrative.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'mmmmmm tentatively upvoting, I suppose.

It's got three pretty simple narrative elements: 1) a document through which 2) an entity is screwing with the Foundation via 3) doomed neutralization ideas.

It's a simple narrative and most of the space is taken up by the proposals, but I think it works. (It's rare for me to enjoy someone writing hate notes to the Foundation, so that's an achievement.) Also, I think there's a good balance with the crosstesting, with you providing enough additional narrative that you aren't just riding their coat-tails.

The image and the lulz explanation nearly killed it for me before I got into the meat. I mean, makes sense to have an insultingly childish cover in context, but it still threw it off.

I have some more in-universe issues (For example, if the documents are Level 4 or higher (so O5) and they think the SCP details are accurate, who do they have reading this?), but I don't know of addressing them world help.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I read the whole thing and I'm still not sure what the point of it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Uh… it's a first, yes. I am oddly intrigued enough to want to write some of these records myself. However, that's just that – it's a bunch of papers that puts down neutralisation proposals and with little context to it.

EDIT: Now that I think it is slowly gaining traction, please open up for collab. I will admit that I will likely upvote the collab while neutral-vote this one, in this case.

item.title: YOLO
item.content:encoded:
Image was made by me.

Will add a collab log if this is not downvoted into oblivion.
